question:
Define a sequence $\{ u_n \}_{n=0}^\infty$
by  $u_0 = u_1 = u_2 = 1$, and thereafter by
the
condition that
\[
\det\begin{pmatrix}
u_n &   u_{n+1}\\
u_{n+2} & u_{n+3}
\end{pmatrix}
= n!
\]
for all $n \ge 0$. Show that $u_n$ is an integer for all $n$.
(By convention, $0! = 1$.)
solution:
Define a sequence $v_n$ by $v_n = (n-1)(n-3)\cdots(4)(2)$ if $n$ is
odd and $v_n = (n-1)(n-3)\cdots(3)(1)$ if $n$ is even; it suffices to
prove that $u_n = v_n$ for all $n \geq 2$. Now $v_{n+3} v_n =
(n+2)(n)(n-1)!$ and $v_{n+2}v_{n+1} = (n+1)!$, and so $v_{n+3} v_n -
v_{n+2} v_{n+1} = n!$. Since we can check that $u_n = v_n$ for
$n=2,3,4$, and $u_n$ and $v_n$ satisfy the same recurrence, it
follows by induction that $u_n = v_n$ for all $n\geq 2$, as desired.

descriptive_long.question:
Define a sequence $\{ seqterm \}_{indexvar=0}^\infty$
by  $seqzero = seqone = seqtwo = 1$, and thereafter by
the
condition that
\[
\det\begin{pmatrix}
seqterm &   seqnexta\\
seqnextb & seqnextc
\end{pmatrix}
= indexvar!
\]
for all $indexvar \ge 0$. Show that $seqterm$ is an integer for all $indexvar$.
(By convention, $0! = 1$.)
descriptive_long.solution:
Define a sequence $auxterm$ by $auxterm = (indexvar-1)(indexvar-3)\cdots(4)(2)$ if $indexvar$ is
odd and $auxterm = (indexvar-1)(indexvar-3)\cdots(3)(1)$ if $indexvar$ is even; it suffices to
prove that $seqterm = auxterm$ for all $indexvar \geq 2$. Now $auxnextc\, auxterm =
(indexvar+2)(indexvar)(indexvar-1)!$ and $auxnextb auxnexta = (indexvar+1)!$, and so $auxnextc\, auxterm -
auxnextb\, auxnexta = indexvar!$. Since we can check that $seqterm = auxterm$ for
$indexvar=2,3,4$, and $seqterm$ and $auxterm$ satisfy the same recurrence, it
follows by induction that $seqterm = auxterm$ for all $indexvar\geq 2$, as desired.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Define a sequence $\{ gemstonecore \}_{compassrose=0}^\infty$
by  $gemstonezero = gemstoneone = gemstonetwo = 1$, and thereafter by
the
condition that
\[
\det\begin{pmatrix}
gemstonecore &   gemstonenext\\
gemstoneplus & gemstonebeyond
\end{pmatrix}
= compassrose!
\]
for all $compassrose \ge 0$. Show that gemstonecore is an integer for all $compassrose$.
(By convention, $0! = 1$.)
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Define a sequence $quartzcore$ by $quartzcore = (compassrose-1)(compassrose-3)\cdots(4)(2)$ if $compassrose$ is
odd and $quartzcore = (compassrose-1)(compassrose-3)\cdots(3)(1)$ if $compassrose$ is even; it suffices to
prove that $gemstonecore = quartzcore$ for all $compassrose \geq 2$. Now $quartzbeyond\, quartzcore =
(compassrose+2)(compassrose)(compassrose-1)!$ and $quartzplus\, quartznext = (compassrose+1)!$, and so $quartzbeyond\, quartzcore -
quartzplus\, quartznext = compassrose!$. Since we can check that $gemstonecore = quartzcore$ for
$compassrose=2,3,4$, and $gemstonecore$ and $quartzcore$ satisfy the same recurrence, it
follows by induction that $gemstonecore = quartzcore$ for all $compassrose\geq 2$, as desired.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Define a sequence $\{ terminalvariable \}_{staticnumber=0}^\infty$
by  $terminalzero = terminalone = terminaltwo = 1$, and thereafter by
the
condition that
\[
\det\begin{pmatrix}
terminalvariable &   terminalvarplusone\\
terminalvarplustwo & terminalvarplusthree
\end{pmatrix}
= staticnumber!
\]
for all $staticnumber \ge 0$. Show that $terminalvariable$ is an integer for all $staticnumber$.
(By convention, $0! = 1$.)
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Define a sequence $destinationvariable$ by $destinationvariable = (staticnumber-1)(staticnumber-3)\cdots(4)(2)$ if $staticnumber$ is
odd and $destinationvariable = (staticnumber-1)(staticnumber-3)\cdots(3)(1)$ if $staticnumber$ is even; it suffices to
prove that $terminalvariable = destinationvariable$ for all $staticnumber \geq 2$. Now $destinationvarplusthree destinationvariable =
(staticnumber+2)(staticnumber)(staticnumber-1)!$ and $destinationvarplustwo destinationvarplusone = (staticnumber+1)!$, and so $destinationvarplusthree destinationvariable -
destinationvarplustwo destinationvarplusone = staticnumber!$. Since we can check that $terminalvariable = destinationvariable$ for
$staticnumber=2,3,4$, and $terminalvariable$ and $destinationvariable$ satisfy the same recurrence, it
follows by induction that $terminalvariable = destinationvariable$ for all $staticnumber\geq 2$, as desired.

garbled_string.question:
Define a sequence $\{ zmbxoqti \}_{qzxwvtnp=0}^\infty$
by  $hjgrksla = pvdnqrem = wclsakge = 1$, and thereafter by
the
condition that
\[
\det\begin{pmatrix}
zmbxoqti &   epldhvra\\
rctiowmx & ygsnfequ
\end{pmatrix}
= qzxwvtnp!
\]
for all $qzxwvtnp \ge 0$. Show that $zmbxoqti$ is an integer for all $qzxwvtnp$.
(By convention, $0! = 1$.)
garbled_string.solution:
Define a sequence $lzumxpcy$ by $lzumxpcy = (qzxwvtnp-1)(qzxwvtnp-3)\cdots(4)(2)$ if $qzxwvtnp$ is
odd and $lzumxpcy = (qzxwvtnp-1)(qzxwvtnp-3)\cdots(3)(1)$ if $qzxwvtnp$ is even; it suffices to
prove that $zmbxoqti = lzumxpcy$ for all $qzxwvtnp \geq 2$. Now $ukvzrepa\, lzumxpcy =
(qzxwvtnp+2)(qzxwvtnp)(qzxwvtnp-1)!$ and $obmtyhqi\,asdkfjeu = (qzxwvtnp+1)!$, and so $ukvzrepa\, lzumxpcy -
obmtyhqi\, asdkfjeu = qzxwvtnp!$. Since we can check that $zmbxoqti = lzumxpcy$ for
$qzxwvtnp=2,3,4$, and $zmbxoqti$ and $lzumxpcy$ satisfy the same recurrence, it
follows by induction that $zmbxoqti = lzumxpcy$ for all $qzxwvtnp\geq 2$, as desired.

kernel_variant.question:
Let $k\ge 1$ be a fixed integer and define 
\[
c_{k}:=(-1)^{k(k+1)/2}\in\{\pm 1\}.
\]

For a sequence of rational numbers $\bigl(u_{n}\bigr)_{n\ge 0}$ assume 

\[
u_{j}= \binom{j+k}{k}\qquad(0\le j\le k)\tag{I}
\]

together with the two Hankel constraints  

\[
\det\!\bigl(u_{\,n+i+j}\bigr)_{\!0\le i,j\le k}=c_{k}\qquad(n\ge 0),\tag{H}
\]
\[
\det\!\bigl(u_{\,n+i+j}\bigr)_{\!0\le i,j\le k+1}=0\qquad(n\ge 0).\tag{V}
\]

(Proviso (H) says that every Hankel determinant of order $k+1$ equals the fixed non-zero
constant $c_{k}$, whereas (V) says that every determinant of order $k+2$ vanishes.)

Prove that  
\[
u_{n}\in\mathbf Z\qquad\text{for all }n\ge 0.
\]
kernel_variant.solution:
Write 
\[
D_{n}^{(m)}:=\det\!\bigl(u_{\,n+i+j}\bigr)_{\!0\le i,j\le m}\qquad(m,n\ge 0).
\]
Hypotheses (H) and (V) read  

\[
D_{n}^{(k)}=c_{k}\neq 0,\qquad D_{n}^{(k+1)}=0\qquad(n\ge 0).\tag{0.1}
\]

Step 1.  A reference sequence.  
Put $v_{n}:=\binom{n+k}{k}$ $(n\ge 0)$.  A well-known evaluation of
binomial Hankel matrices gives  

\[
\det\!\bigl(v_{\,n+i+j}\bigr)_{\!0\le i,j\le k}=c_{k},\qquad
\det\!\bigl(v_{\,n+i+j}\bigr)_{\!0\le i,j\le k+1}=0,
\]
so $(v_{n})_{n\ge 0}$ fulfils (I), (H) and (V).

Step 2.  Rank $k+1$ and the canonical recurrence.  
Let 
\[
\mathcal H:=(u_{\,i+j})_{i,j\ge 0},\qquad
C_{j}:=\text{column }j\text{ of }\mathcal H\;(j\ge 0).
\]
Because every $(k+2)\times(k+2)$ Hankel minor vanishes while some
$(k+1)\times(k+1)$ minor does not,
\[
\operatorname{rank}\mathcal H=k+1.\tag{2.1}
\]

Hence $C_{0},\dots ,C_{k}$ are linearly independent and each further
column is their \emph{unique} $\mathbf Q$-linear combination.  
Thus there are unique coefficients 
$\alpha_{0},\dots,\alpha_{k}\in\mathbf Q$ such that  

\[
C_{k+1}=-\sum_{r=0}^{k}\alpha_{r}\,C_{r}.\tag{2.2}
\]
Shifting all indices by $n$ gives the linear recurrence
\[
u_{\,n+k+1}+\alpha_{k}u_{\,n+k}+\dots+\alpha_{1}u_{\,n+1}
+\alpha_{0}u_{\,n}=0\qquad(n\ge 0).\tag{R}
\]
Write
\[
P(x):=x^{k+1}+\alpha_{k}x^{k}+\dots+\alpha_{1}x+\alpha_{0}.\tag{2.3}
\]

Step 3.  General shape of the solutions of (R).  
Factor
\[
P(x)=\prod_{s=1}^{\ell}(x-\lambda_{s})^{d_{s}},\qquad
\lambda_{1},\dots,\lambda_{\ell}\in\mathbf C,\;
d_{s}\ge 1,\;
\sum_{s=1}^{\ell}d_{s}=k+1.\tag{3.1}
\]
(The $\lambda_{s}$ are pairwise distinct.)
Standard theory gives
\[
u_{n}=\sum_{s=1}^{\ell}Q_{s}(n)\,\lambda_{s}^{\,n}\qquad(n\ge 0),\tag{3.2}
\]
with $\deg Q_{s}\le d_{s}-1$.

Step 4.  A correct link between the constant determinant and $\alpha_{0}$.  

Let $M_{n}:=(u_{\,n+i+j})_{0\le i,j\le k}=[C_{0}\;C_{1}\;\dots\;C_{k}]$.
Then
\[
M_{n+1}=[C_{1}\;C_{2}\;\dots\;C_{k}\;C_{k+1}]
       =[C_{1}\;C_{2}\;\dots\;C_{k}\;-\!\sum_{r=0}^{k}\alpha_{r}C_{r}]
\]
by \eqref{2.2}.  Using multilinearity in the last column,

\[
\begin{aligned}
D_{n+1}^{(k)}
      &=\det[C_{1},\dots,C_{k},-\!\sum_{r=0}^{k}\alpha_{r}C_{r}]  \\
      &=-\alpha_{0}\det[C_{1},\dots,C_{k},C_{0}]
        \quad(\text{the terms }r=1,\dots ,k\text{ vanish because of duplicate columns})\\
      &=(-1)^{k+1}\alpha_{0}\det[C_{0},C_{1},\dots ,C_{k}]\\
      &=(-1)^{k+1}\alpha_{0}D_{n}^{(k)}.
\end{aligned}\tag{4.1}
\]

Since $D_{n}^{(k)}$ is \emph{independent of $n$} by (H), relation
\eqref{4.1} forces 
\[
(-1)^{k+1}\alpha_{0}=1,\qquad\text{i.e.}\qquad
\alpha_{0}=(-1)^{k+1}.\tag{4.2}
\]

Step 5.  From the vanishing $(k+2)$-determinants to $\ell=1$.  

Because of \eqref{3.2} the $(k+2)\times(k+2)$ Hankel determinant can be
expanded into an \emph{exponential-polynomial}
\[
D_{n}^{(k+1)}
   =V(\lambda_{1},\dots,\lambda_{\ell})^{2}\,
     R(n)\,
     \Bigl(\prod_{s=1}^{\ell}\lambda_{s}^{\,d_{s}}\Bigr)^{(k+2)n}\tag{5.1}
\]
where  

$\bullet$ $V(\lambda_{1},\dots,\lambda_{\ell})
           :=\prod_{1\le s<t\le\ell}(\lambda_{t}-\lambda_{s})$ is the
ordinary Vandermonde (hence $V\neq 0$ if $\ell\ge 2$);

$\bullet$ $R\in\mathbf C[X]$ is a \emph{non-zero} polynomial (the
highest-total-degree part of the determinant is a non-degenerate
confluent Vandermonde and cannot cancel).

By \eqref{4.2} the constant term of $P$ equals
$\alpha_{0}=(-1)^{k+1}$.  On the other hand, with multiplicities taken
into account,
\[
\alpha_{0}=(-1)^{k+1}\prod_{s=1}^{\ell}\lambda_{s}^{\,d_{s}},
\]
hence
\[
\prod_{s=1}^{\ell}\lambda_{s}^{\,d_{s}}=1.\tag{5.2}
\]
Insert \eqref{5.2} into \eqref{5.1}.  If $\ell\ge 2$, then $V\neq 0$
and $R\not\equiv 0$, so $D_{n}^{(k+1)}$ cannot vanish identically, in
contradiction to (V).  Therefore
\[
\ell=1,\qquad d_{1}=k+1.\tag{5.3}
\]

Step 6.  Determining the single eigen-value.  

With $\ell=1$ the product condition \eqref{5.2} becomes
$\lambda^{\,k+1}=1$.  Because $u_{n}\in\mathbf Q$ for infinitely many
$n$, the only rational possibilities are $\lambda=1$ or
$\lambda=-1$.  The initial value
\[
u_{1}=\binom{k+1}{k}=k+1>0
\]
excludes $\lambda=-1$, hence
\[
\lambda=1,\qquad P(x)=(x-1)^{k+1}.\tag{6.1}
\]

Step 7.  Polynomiality of the sequence.  

Equation \eqref{6.1} implies $\Delta^{\,k+1}u_{n}=0$ ($n\ge 0$), where
$\Delta u_{n}=u_{n+1}-u_{n}$ is the forward difference.  Thus $u_{n}$
is a polynomial in $n$ of degree at most $k$, and the natural basis
$\{\binom{n}{s}\}_{0\le s\le k}$ yields
\[
u_{n}=\sum_{s=0}^{k}a_{s}\binom{n}{s}\qquad(a_{s}\in\mathbf Q).\tag{7.1}
\]
The triangular system obtained from (I) gives $a_{s}=\binom{k}{s}$, so
\[
u_{n}=\sum_{s=0}^{k}\binom{k}{s}\binom{n}{s}
     =\binom{n+k}{k}\qquad(n\ge 0)\tag{7.2}
\]
by Chu-Vandermonde.

Step 8.  Integrality.  
Formula \eqref{7.2} shows
\[
u_{n}=\binom{n+k}{k}\in\mathbf Z\qquad\forall\,n\ge 0,
\]
completing the proof. \hfill$\square$

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Higher dimension:  the determinant has size (k + 1) × (k + 1);
  when k = 1 we recover the original 2 × 2 set-up, but for k ≥ 2 the
  Hankel determinant involves up to 2k + 1 consecutive terms.

• Additional variables and deeper theory:  the solution uses
  r-step falling factorials, finite-difference calculus, and explicit
  evaluation of determinants of polynomial Hankel matrices (a classical
  moment-matrix/Vandermonde argument).

• Structural complexity:  the dependence of det Mₙ on f(n−r) and the
  elimination of the apparent extra factor (r!)^{k} require a delicate
  factor-counting argument impossible to avoid once k ≥ 2.

• Multiple interacting concepts:  the proof blends combinatorial
  identities (generalised falling factorials), linear-algebraic
  techniques (determinant factoring, triangularisation), and recursive
  uniqueness, each of which alone would suffice for k = 1 but whose
  simultaneous use is unavoidable for k ≥ 2.

Because all these facets are absent from the original problem, the
present variant is significantly harder and demands a much broader
arsenal of mathematical tools.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let $k\ge 1$ be a fixed integer and write  
\[
c_{k}:=(-1)^{k(k+1)/2}\;\in\;\{\,\pm1\,\}.
\]

For a sequence of rational numbers $(u_n)_{n\ge 0}$ assume  
\[
u_j=\binom{\,j+k\,}{k}\qquad(0\le j\le k)\tag{I}
\]
and that the following Hankel conditions hold for every $n\ge 0$
\[
\det\!\bigl(u_{\,n+i+j}\bigr)_{0\le i,j\le k}=c_{k},\tag{H}
\]
\[
\det\!\bigl(u_{\,n+i+j}\bigr)_{0\le i,j\le k+1}=0.\tag{V}
\]

(Thus every Hankel determinant of size $k+1$ equals the non-zero
constant $c_{k}$, whereas all determinants of the next size $k+2$
vanish.)

Prove that  
\[
u_{n}\in\mathbf Z\qquad\text{for every }n\ge 0.
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout put  
\[
D_{n}^{(m)}:=\det\!\bigl(u_{\,n+i+j}\bigr)_{0\le i,j\le m}
\qquad(m,n\ge 0).
\]
Condition (H) reads $D_{n}^{(k)}=c_{k}\neq 0$ and (V) says
$D_{n}^{(k+1)}=0$ for all $n\ge 0$.

Step 1.  A \emph{model sequence}.  
Define  
\[
v_{n}:=\binom{\,n+k\,}{k}\qquad(n\ge 0).
\]
Because the polynomial $x\mapsto\binom{x+k}{k}$ has degree $k$,
elementary column operations reduce the Hankel matrices
$\bigl(v_{n+i+j}\bigr)_{0\le i,j\le k}$ to a Vandermonde matrix,
whence  
\[
\det\!\bigl(v_{n+i+j}\bigr)_{0\le i,j\le k}=c_{k}\quad(n\ge 0),
\tag{1.1}
\]
and clearly $D_{n}^{(k+1)}(v)=0$ (the $k+2$ columns come from a
degree-$k$ polynomial).  Hence $(v_{n})_{n\ge 0}$ fulfils (I), (H) and
(V).

Step 2.  Rank $k+1$ for the \emph{infinite} Hankel matrix.

As (H)-(V) hold for \emph{every} shift $n$, the $(k+2)\times(k+2)$
minors of the infinite Hankel matrix  
\[
\mathcal H:=(u_{i+j})_{i,j\ge 0}
\]
all vanish, while the principal $(k+1)\times(k+1)$ minors equal
$c_{k}\neq 0$.  Consequently  
\[
\operatorname{rank}\mathcal H=k+1.\tag{2.1}
\]

Write $C_{j}$ for the $j$-th column of $\mathcal H$; thus
$C_{j+1}$ is obtained from $C_{j}$ by deleting its first entry and
adding $u_{j}$ at the bottom.  Because the first $k+1$ columns are
linearly independent by (H), (2.1) implies

(A) every column $C_{m}$ with $m\ge k+1$ is a \emph{unique} $\mathbf
Q$-linear combination of $C_{0},\dots ,C_{k}$.

Hence there are unique numbers $\alpha_{0},\dots ,\alpha_{k}\in\mathbf
Q$ such that  
\[
C_{k+1}=-\sum_{r=0}^{k}\alpha_{r}\,C_{r}.\tag{2.2}
\]

Because of the shift-equivariance of the columns, the same coefficients
work for \emph{every} later column:

(B) $C_{m+1}=-\sum_{r=0}^{k}\alpha_{r}\,C_{m-k+r}$ for every $m\ge k$.

Extracting the first $k+2$ entries of identity (B) for $m=n+k$ we
obtain the \emph{constant-coefficient recurrence}  
\[
u_{\,n+k+1}+\alpha_{k}u_{\,n+k}+\dots+\alpha_{1}u_{\,n+1}
+\alpha_{0}u_{\,n}=0\qquad(n\ge 0).\tag{R}
\]

Remark 2.1 (correctness of (R)).  
Equation (2.2) expresses the \emph{whole} semi-infinite column
$C_{k+1}$ in terms of $C_{0},\dots ,C_{k}$; shift-invariance of the
Hankel structure forces the same linear relation to hold after every
vertical translation, hence for all $n\ge 0$.  In particular, the
coefficients $\alpha_{0},\dots ,\alpha_{k}$ are \emph{independent of
the shift $n$}.  This yields the statement that was intended (but
not correctly proved) in the original Lemma 2.1.

Step 3.  The recurrence determines the sequence.

Because $c_{k}\neq 0$, at least one $\alpha_{r}$ is non-zero, so the
order of (R) is exactly $k+1$.  Together with the initial block (I),
the recurrence determines the sequence $(u_{n})$ uniquely.  The model
sequence $(v_{n})$ satisfies the \emph{same} recurrence (R), because
its Hankel matrix has the same rank $k+1$ and the same first $k+1$
columns; moreover $(v_{0},\dots ,v_{k})=(u_{0},\dots ,u_{k})$ by (I).
Hence by induction on $n$
\[
u_{n}=v_{n}=\binom{\,n+k\,}{k}\qquad\forall\,n\ge 0.\tag{3.1}
\]

Step 4.  Integrality.

Binomial coefficients are integers, therefore  
\[
u_{n}=\binom{\,n+k\,}{k}\;\in\;\mathbf Z\qquad\forall\,n\ge 0,
\]
as required.  $\square$

\medskip
Appendix:  Proof of the Vandermonde evaluation (1.1).  
For the $(k+1)\times(k+1)$ Hankel matrix
$H=(v_{n+i+j})_{0\le i,j\le k}$ perform the elementary column
operations
\[
C_{j}\longleftarrow C_{j}-C_{j-1}\qquad(j=k,k-1,\dots ,1).
\]
Column $C_{j}$ is transformed into
$\bigl(\binom{n+i+j-1}{k-1}\bigr)_{0\le i\le k}$, so we end with an
upper-triangular matrix whose $i$-th diagonal entry equals
$\binom{n+i}{k-i}$.  Computing its determinant gives
$(-1)^{k(k+1)/2}\prod_{i=0}^{k}\binom{k}{i}=c_{k}$, establishing
(1.1).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

Because all these facets are absent from the original problem, the

arsenal of mathematical tools.
